channel.title: Comments for page "Project Kenowhere Central Volunteer Hub"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Project Kenowhere Central Volunteer Hub"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This hits differently now, for a lot of reasons. When I first read it, two years ago, I wasn't an anarchist. And Kenosha, Wisconsin, had never been in the national news. It hits very differently now. It remains my favorite work on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you liked the article so much! It seems like you really understand it and connect with it and I would be delighted and honored if you translated it. I did not expect that anyone would want to translate this after making it so long so this is a pleasant surprise. I hope your new job is going well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This might be the first long feedback i am leaving for a skip. A very, very well written skip.

Today is the second day of my job. It had nothing to do what I have studied in my university. But i like it, because i believe it can help people.

I am not saying that i know how the main character feels in this story, but i can relate. Destiny calls, and he decides to stay and help. I guess this is a simple concept, but it is hard to write it well, and it is definitely safe to say the author has nailed this one.

Just earlier today i helped to translate an article to Chinese. I would like to do the same for this scip. If not to pay tribute to the author, at least i will remember how much i like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
realized i never commented on this piece
it's so fucking impressive and i love it. i cried very hard the first time i read it when i saw it in newly created and i still don't know how to summarize my feelings on it
i return to this article a lot
thanks dsc and scar

item.title: Reasoning for my vote
item.content:encoded:
As much as I liked the idea of the article, I downvoted it and I'd like to explain why. The main reason why I downvoted is that the article doesn't have an in-universe explanation as to why it has a format screw. If there was an explanation, I didn't catch it. Whenever I read SCP articles, I assume that whatever I'm presented with is how the article appears in the in-universe database for said SCP entry (with some exceptions here and there, such as SCP-2317).

The other issue I have with this article (and the other reason why I downvoted it I think) is that the SCP part doesn't really add anything to the overall article. A part of me feels like it was added so that the article could be part of the mainlist and be considered an SCP rather than it being a Tale or GoI format. The SCP part could be taken out and the article wouldn't suffer from it since I feel like there isn't enough to keep it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh boy can't wait it's declass

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am working on an orphanage SCP, and when I am less tired and re-read this, I think I should cross-reference it with this one. They don't class because while the children on Kenowhere don't have parents, they do have guardians, and the orphanage isn't omniscient or omnipresent, though it does its best through purpose, and with direction of its Staff. But yeah, interesting read, will need to reread when not passing out tired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am working on an orphanage SCP, and when I am less tired and re-read this, I think I should cross-reference it with this one. They don't class because while the children on Kenowhere don't have parents, they do have guardians, and the orphanage isn't omniscient or omnipresent, though it does its best through purpose, and with direction of its Staff. But yeah, interesting read, will need to reread when not passing out tired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am sure they would love to have you there :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: anybody else here wishing kenowhere were real? I sure wish it was a real place. I'm not sure what I could provide as a disabled person but… I guess I'd figure it out!! I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is really, really lovely

i'd like to go

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: masterpiece! i can't say enough about this skip to convince you of how much i love it, but i can say my favorite part was the bit when Mr. Destiny's head took on the form of the dog greenmanwalking accidentally hit when he was a kid. this is just… so heartwarming, and written so skillfully it's hard to not at least consider it my favorite SCP on the website. beautiful work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Deadass almost cried. You two are now the closest anyone's gotten to getting me to cry on this website. (How far I've come! But not now, Deadcanons, not now.)

I'm not sure if Destiny is supposed to be Anubis or not, but I'm headcanoning him that way at the very least. Just a few weeks ago I was thinking to myself about how… lacking the Egyptian lore is around here, and I was wondering what an Anubis SCP would look like. I kept getting stuck on how overused and trope-y he's become; how can such an oft-referenced figure function effectively in this universe?

You… absolutely fucking nailed it. I'm glad I didn't come up with an answer to that question, because yours is way better than whatever I would have produced. Because he's not JUST Anubis, he's all sorts of different things. That interpretation is just one facet of the character. And he's both extremely violent and creepy, but also genuinely kind and wise. God. Fucking amazing. I could go on about Destiny alone for a while.

The rest of the article is just excellent. Great characterization across the board. The writing is lovely and easy to fall into. Its long, but it never feels it.

The 5+ titles are great, and I love your use of the corrupted SCP number on the mainlist. Very cool meta idea to have an SCP that is also a Hub. There's just… so much about this I love. Huge +1, and I'd make it a +2 or maybe +5 if I could. Or maybe a +100.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i can't believe i never read this until now! this article is like, legit one of, if not the, best scps i've ever read. i love gaw stuff and this just elevates gaw even more. i especially love the neat touch of gaw making a mister that helps people find their purpose and then said mister immediately (and maybe even retroactively) helping them find theirs. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After reading this entry (took me several hours), I needed a long pause. Made me rethink what it means to be human. Kudos.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was incredibly well written and well thought out, I love this! Definitely going to be drawing some fan art of it, just so many amazing moments in the greenmanwalking reports that made the characters feel so human and real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't like the format screw. I don't think it looks justified. I really dislike format screw for the sake of format screw. I don't think it looks justified. I really dislike format screw for the sake of format screw. It reads as format screw for the sake of getting a tale on the main list. The SCP format tab looks like it could have been removed and the story it tells would be completely unchanged. I will note that I haven't actually read it yet and this is all based on my initial impressions of it. After reading it I might like it but my first impressions are of dislike. I like SCP articles to have a fairly predictable format and tales to be where the majority of the creativity is at, personally. I don't like it when it is a struggle to determine what an SCP object actually is. Like, I clicked on the page and I was startled. I thought I somehow ended up on the wrong page.

Edit after reading it:
Alright, I've read it. I think I liked it. I still have my reservations about it being a tale in an SCP slot but I liked it.

Edit after thinking about it some more:
I upvoted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is probably one of my favourite articles on this website.

I'm no religious user of the site. I actually know very little about any of the GoIs and frequently forget that they exist while I Random-SCP my way through the night, occasionally stopping to read a good tale, but this article has made me want to know m o r e .

I especially liked the use of the footnotes, seemed to me like a very effective way of humanizing the GAW characters, and moreover it was actually funny (maybe that's like a trope of GAW articles or something though, I dunno like I said I'm no GoI expert).
I also like the perspective used.

One thing I wanna mention also is that the foundation's article was corrupted and unreadable for good reason in my opinion, because using the GOC one instead lent a lot more to the story and the GAW archives was a unique and interesting format.

Listing this as a tale wouldn't have given it the same feel, really, since it is after all an archival account of what the SCP is and does. The extensions on that, like the 'how you can help' section are no more out of place than certain interview or exploration logs are on regularly-formatted articles, especially since Kenowhere is acting as a -1 to the SCP.

All in all, good job. I wanted to add some criticism here but can't really think of anything right now, I'll try harder

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm inclined to agree that Mr. Destiny's little speech there is a little too heavy on praising GAW and dismissing other groups but it should be understood as

  1. A polemic directed at an ideological rival.
  2. An exasperated 'are you even looking for reasons I'd do this and not reasons why I'm wrong?' statement.

more than it should be understood as our views as authors (or, really, even Mr. Destiny's complete views) on the actual nature of those groups. I do feel a little guilty about being mean to the Serpent's Hand. I don't have specific intentions to alter that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not what I was thinking of but it works, yeah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this to be an incredible read, and enjoyed almost the entire journey through it.

Below are my main critiques of the format screw.

+ Show 
- Hide

The only downside is, as a few have pointed out, the SCP tab feels like a backdoor, shoehorned in way to get it on the mainlist. It should instead exist to contribute to the tone and theme of the article. If the SCP tab were removed, it would be no different than any other Tale. You could have had an SCP article integrated more intimately into the overall story. An example could be the final tab being a SCP document of a successfully contained 4239 after the Foundation has dismantled everything about Kenowhere, it's occupants detained / amenitized and some released, and a final interview with greenmanwalking. This would be an incredibly sad ending and gut punch after building up such an optimistic article. It would most importantly justify itself as a mainlist entry.

Or as another example that is incredibly simple: the Foundation views the anomoly as semi-contained Euclid entity (because it is), that they are researching long term containment options (because thats what they do) and is on standby for clean up incase GAW's illusionary methods fails (less resource intensive than trying to stop GAW and putting their own shroud over the whole thing before they have a rock solid game plan). It would be an interesting way to show the Foundation working "in real time" trying to contain Kenowhere and GAW. It would be a nice look at the discretion they exercise in the early stages of containing an object, since any action they take would probably be more disasterous for containment than sitting back, monitoring GAW, and coming up with a longer term strategy.

Having an actual SCP tab with something that adds to the article would justify it on the mainlist. Right now it's just an unjustified format screw.

That said, on it's own merits, it was an exceptional piece and one of the better longer form tales or stories I've had the opportunity of reading on this sites in the years I've been lurking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realised why Destiny's head keeps changing, after reading 4669 too.

When GAW made him, they wanted something with a dog head.
But even though they may have been inspired by Anubis, Anubis specifically has a jackal head.

They never specified which dog Destiny should get his head from. So he just has a generic 'dog' head that changes now and then.

item.title: I'm sweating through my eyes
item.content:encoded:
Goddamn I can't tell if my eyes are watering from eye strain or if I'm crying. This is beautiful. Just actually genuinely beautiful. I haven't had this kind of reaction to anything on this site since reading the Thirty Six and seeing the 001/076 test log.

GAW were never one of my favorite GOIs. That may change, if this kind of work keeps getting produced. It's not even about Destiny, it's about GAW's reaction to him. They have a duty, now, and a goal. He gave them a goal, and they rose to the occasion. The choice to draw a recruit from the GOC was inspired. The KTE designation is even more code-wordified and impersonal than the SCP designation, due to the lack of familiarity with it for most site users, and the way the GOC treats anomalies means that greenmanwalking has to travel even further, personally, to accept Destiny and GAW than an SCP agent would.

This is fundamentally a character piece for greenmanwalking and GAW as a whole. Something like this needed to be on the site, and I'm glad it's been done so well, and so profoundly.

+OVERFLOW_ERROR

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This… is a thing, certainly.

Concurring with Kirby upthread that both the corrupted SCP file and the mainlist spot this uses feel unnecessary. This wouldn't be any less monumental and "epic" in the classical sense were it tagged as tale and the tab removed.

That said. The core conceit here, of Kenowhere itself, is very nice, and finally solves an annoying poke of fridge logic that always bugged me in GAW stuff: "if they're so magic and their home life sucks, why don't they just leave?" Hopefully this piece will mean the abandonment of that tired plot thread for future GAW works in favor of fresher directions. The help tab is probably my favorite part, honestly, for the sheer thought that had to go into it, as well as the mental image of some poor outcast with the power to summon MRI parts or copper tubes finally getting a day in the spotlight.

The change of heart in the GOC dude was also neat to follow, and beautifully paced and doled out without excessive cheese or heavy-handedness. Also appreciated were all the cameo members who truly wanted to better themselves and help others.

However: I'm pretty sick of "Only X group gets it and all the other groups are stupid/ignorant" as a plot point in the last of the greenman logs, and making it so that the main chat peeps select who gets to come in, while sensible in-universe, clashes with the aura of Mr. Destiny itself by making Kenowhere feel a little bit more like an exclusive club rather than a haven.

I don't know how to feel about the entire thing overall yet. We'll see. I finished it despite not liking long stuff, so I guess that's something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Earth Shrugs Off Atlas

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Fortune Redistribution Project is the project which created Mr. Destiny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never thought of it like that. Mr. Destiny is anti John Galt. Kenowhere is anti Galt’s Gulch. I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this article is like SCP-2000, but for GAW instead of the Foundation….? That is, it takes a bunch of the running themes and motifs of GAW, brings them together into one article in a way that's beautiful, and that gives all those themes a sense of belonging together. It also gives the overall organization a greater sense of purpose and gravitas, like they're not just "a bunch of scattered anomalies" (for the Foundation) or "a buncha magic internet folk", but they're actually working towards something greater than they are, something beyond them.

I'm kinda rambling, but my point is that I really fuckin love this article, both on its own and how it affects the greater GAW canon.

I'll also say that I appreciated the "How you can help" section, which grounds the piece in reality. It makes the call for help feel that much more real, since it's so close to how things are with existing charities, etc.

My one critique is that the SCP-ℸp♡9 tab is kind of superfluous and isn't really followed-up on. Like, the corrupted tab isn't really explored any further, and presents little information/narrative build-up as-is. The "Fortune Redistribution Project" is also mentioned but never elaborated, but I guess that's just some other group's archive of Kenowhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Soo —- it's the inverse of Atlas Shrugged.

I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this touched my heart and brought me comfort. it was earnest and uplifting on a site where you can't find much of that. this is all the best parts of GAW, all in one place.

the christmas phone was my favorite little detail in an article full of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this. GAW are still their weird, entirely relatable selves, but there's a sense of *importance* here.

Also I giggle madly imagining them going "Okay we need someone to actually be serious and collect all this information." and everyone just chorusing "bones." because it's the serious one. Really, this just sells the idea that a completely nonanomalous girl and a giant space clam reformed bioweapon ended up being the two anchors for the group and I love it.

Even if this whole article really just makes me want to hang out with them even more. I mean the magic stuff is cool and all but really I just think the entire group sounds like my kind of people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the most pleasing compliment I've gotten on the article given how much I struggled to make you like the draft of it from a year ago. (You weren't being ruthless or anything; it just wasn't your thing and that led to you pointing out a lot of important parts that people who liked the draft more as it was didn't see.) I kept saying to my friends, I kept saying, "I won't be satisfied until ARD gets it and likes it." and they kept telling me that that was never going to happen and you can't please everyone so I dropped it until now and I mean, yeah, alright, I didn't get 100% upvotes on this, but

I did get yours. That's my victory. Hope that doesn't feel weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can I just say how great Jude pointing out how most misters against weed don't want anything to do with GAW is? Like, Mr. Meme actually works with the janitors! Lol great article, glad I finally joined the site to be able to upvote it! (0w0✿)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am hopeful that this and SCP-4669 signify a more mature take on the group - that they're finally growing up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one of the most beautiful pieces on the site, and finally sold me on GAW. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… so… much…

many words

such words

like, almost too many words? (I'll get to that)

But like mostly good words.

Critique
– hide block

Ok, so firstly, I don't really vote based off of this, but the Foundation Document feels super shoe-horned in as like a half-excuse to be able to put this in an SCP slot. I didn't get enough out of it to feel like foreshadowing, and like… I feel like the story would've been exactly the same if it weren't there.

Secondly, I like a lot of the "How you can help" tab is somewhat superfluous. Like, it does show a lot of the fact that they care, but I feel like the list of requested materials just drags on and doesn't really go anywhere. Like it didn't really advance the narrative at all.

The anomaly maintenance was clever, but I wish the issues outlined in that part had played into the GOC report, or the greenmanwalking reports much more. Like, it mentions that there are flaws in the system, but those are not really acknowledged nor capitalized earlier on, so it comes off as random exposition when it probably could've been a cool "a-ha" moment if symptoms of the imperfect system was presented in the reports. (or maybe I'm an idiot and I missed the references to it)

Also there were a few times where it felt like the greenmanwalking files were being a bit too poetic for their own good. I understand they're not supposed to be super formal, but some little cute prose pieces that I use when writing tales and such kind of jumped out at me. Like:

Back at its tent, it said to me, "do you understand now what I do here, Silvanus Ayers?"

Back at its tent, I said to it, "no."

This exchange here feels like Ayers is playing up the drama of the moment quite a bit, which feels weird for the reports, at least at his semi-early stage (this is at 5 out of 15 reports).

Finally, you describe the actual effect of Destiny at least twice (and like, half credit to the GOC document) in relatively dense detail. Once in the Summation, and once in the What You Can Do To Help. It felt like I was being beat over the head with the concept "he unlocks your inner potential" at those points.


The reasons I still upvoted
– hide block

The overall structure of this thing is really nice, and the story it tells feels like a breath of fresh air for the GoI. The GOC doc was a nice set up and the greenmanwalking reports were (for the most part) really really nice. I love clear, but also subtle character arcs, and this pulled that off quite nicely. I also really enjoyed the "On Destiny's Call" section. That was a nice little addition too, and brought some more concrete fun to the SCP, grounding what was a very "Look at the power of self-actualization and revel in its presence" up until then.

I really hope that this and the Zapista article are indicative of a new side of GAW being explored. At least I hope these aren't one-offs.

ok now I'm the one with too many words, but no TLDR because if I read your whole thing then you have to read my little response :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh holy shit I remember critting this forever ago and loving it! (Albeit with some reservations about database justification but honestly I don’t give as much of a fuck about that nowadays).

Glad to see this monumental work has finally reached the database!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit, this is above and beyond anything I expected from the beginning. At the end of the fourth tab I actually forgot there was more to read and almost closed the entire thing. The lack of understanding by both the Foundation and the GOC, the slow reveal of what Mr. Destiny actually is, greenmanwalking's journey - this whole story keeps pulling you in without missing a beat.
Series V[footnote]]Featuring Dante from the Devil May Cry series[[/footnote]] is really off to an amazing start.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one my favorites that I've read in a long while. I really love GAW as a concept so I get excited every time I see something relating to them and this… oh wow.

What really got me was the "How You Can Help" tab. Until that point I'd enjoyed it for sure, but seeing the lists of needed materials and items, medicines especially, really hit me. It just made it seem more real? Like yeah, all sorts of anomalous shenanigans are afoot but these people still need to, y'know, eat. The fact that there is an effort to make this a sustainable little town for the people who need to be there just really tugs at me for some reason. My weakness for found-family stories? Probably why I love GAW so much to begin with…

tl;dr I love this dog man and his commune.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, you put a crap ton of work into this article! The monster thing was a Girl Genius webcomic reference, Silas definitely comes off as a cool guy to hang around!

Thank you for writing :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for enjoying our article so much! You had a lot of comments and I'm going to respond to them on an arbitrary basis in reverse order under the following collapsible. Because I am choosing which comments to address arbitrarily, I will, of course, leave some thoughts unremarked on but I think very soon you're going to see why that would have been a length issue. I wrote this post in the middle of the night with nobody around, on ADHD medication (prescription). You may assume from this statement and the size of the post to follow, along with the size of the article, that significant chunks of the article were also written while on ADHD medication at night, but you would not be nearly as right as you think.

You have arrived at the collapsible.
Thank you for opening my words. I sincerely hope you do not regret it.

The context for 'tankfuckers' is the earlier footnote wherein the Global Occult Coalition is referred to as "The other janitors. The mean ones. […] the ones who want to fuck tanks" which is a joke the character gaycopmp4 is making about the fact that the GOC is more openly militarized than the SCP Foundation, whom they have more history of dealing with. It's a way of diminishing the perceived significance of the extensive and frightening military budget by, if nothing else, implying it's more than they really need and on some level they have only militarized so much because of a kind of obsession with things that can destroy. It's a way of diminishing the people who hold that power by saying they're flawed, obsessive, ungrounded in the reality that there are other important things. This is parallel to the socialist views on the role of the military within a capitalist state (and the GOC mainly deals with capitalist states) that many Gamers Against Weed members, including gaycopmp4, display in several other pieces featuring them. This is further based on the tendency I have observed in socialist-dominant online communities to joke about people who are really into Army stuff by saying that they kind of sound like they want to fuck a gun. This is a common form of joke that I myself have engaged in, within the appropriate social contexts. Thank you for asking.

I don't think of Silas as a monster in any capacity. I think he is just an idealistic person who ended up aligned somewhere he didn't want to be because he didn't fully understand what he was getting into. I think he thought of himself as able to grasp the whole 'we're fighting the unknowable' thing because of youthful overconfidence and it left him in an unfortunate position. I think that persisting within that context and taking his chance to escape it when he saw it is what gives him the ability to confront the unfortunate and assume responsibility for an unpleasant task. I don't think it has that much to do with the things he actually did as an armory sergeant. His talents are in communicating with large groups about their needs, prioritizing those needs, finding the best way to meet those needs, and, ultimately, meeting those needs. Silas' talents are for mass nurturing, because that is his primary interest in life. Thereby, the username referencing a centuries-old symbol of life and vitality (The Green Man), and a form of motion (walking). I think of Silas as somewhat low-key pretentious.

hetcopogg is the heart holding the entire Gamers Against Weed organization together and this article was written with that as an axiom. I am glad to see that you agree. I'm also glad you enjoyed our wider use of the word 'pakoosh' as an onomatopoeia capable of representing a wide variety of actions. On the other hand, the explosion idea isn't far off-base. polaricecraps says he 'pakooshes' things in much the same way someone might say they 'slam dunked' or 'crushed' a task that they performed exceedingly well. polaricecraps is a little showy, to the point where it blurs into his general-purpose vocabulary. We observed in research that he is the main 'normal' general-purpose reality bender in the GAW cast (currently) aside from bluntfiend, and, while lacking bluntfiend's miraculous levels of power, his age, and his experience, also lacking Jude Kriyot's disillusionment, depression, and self-loathing. He's pretty enthusiastic about the whole thing, overall. He's pretty enthusiastic about most things. This influenced our word choice.

I, personally, am profoundly pleased that you feel that the article successfully holds the themes of epic quests and magic lands together with the themes of GAW as a GOI. From the start, I considered it the primary goal I had in writing this to reconcile those themes and show they can be linked. From the start, I have considered that the most difficult balancing act of the article, the one to be approached most carefully. It's relieving to find people telling me that, with the right coauthor, I succeeded. The Jock Jams Vol. 6 part was actually the first part of the article I wrote, before I even had the concept of using Mr. Destiny and time shenanigans to make the ancient, immortal creature of the story something that GAW specifically created. It was difficult to puzzle my way through how to do that, but I think it was vital to uniting the themes as they needed to be.

Lastly, no, I have no strong relationship to Wisconsin. I have a good friend who goes to college there and we talk about it sometimes. I courted (not dated) someone from Wisconsin once and still consider them a valued friend. That's it. I know some Wisconsin lore and trivia. I know what the regional convenience stores look like. But I've never been there except driving through, in the back seat, as a child. I don't fully remember at this point, but most likely Silas Ayers comes from the colonial name for the natural wonder/sacred site of Uluru in Australia (Ayers Rock). I am originally from and currently reside in Oregon and I asked for some guidance from a friend when describing Wisconsin, as mentioned in the dedications I gave in my author post. Thank you for reading.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We wrote it with attention deficit disorder, so that may be part of it. Very glad you liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Just one article and I'll go to bed."

Haha, whoops. And now it's 1am on a work night.

No regrets though. Every word was worth it. Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: my attention deficit ass was glued to my seat the whole way through, so you'd have gotten an upvote for that alone

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this! My thoughts are a bit undignified, I started making notes as I read it!

+ My thoughts
- My thoughts

First off-

ON WISCONSIN, ON WISCONSIN, RA RA RA RA RAAAA!
(sorry) Seriously, though, Ayers is a family name on my mom's side… is there another Wisconsinite on this website? :)

I really liked this, there were so many things in it I liked. I never knew that jockjamsvol was Jock Jams Vol 6 - I had been pronouncing the 'vol' like vole. All the little wordbuilding in it was cool. I like how it can feel like this epic quest, this magical land, and still be very much GAW. If I was an old god, I would definitely steal a speedboat and have an epic chase scene with the illuminati. I like the retcon implications- Mr. Destiny's like a nice version of the daevites in that way. I like time hijinks.

Also- I always thought 'pakoosh' was just an explosion, but this gives it a wider definition. I like how hetcopogg gets stuck sneaking Mr. Destiny into Wisconsin. Honestly, I bet she has to do a lot of this kind of crap to help out GAW just because she's "normal."

"We do not need more computers" - sounds about right XD

"polaricecraps: so when harmpit goes down
polaricecraps: very very fuckin slowly" - very nice!

I like how they have the ex-GOC guy doing the interviews- right monster for the right job, yeah?

"It would be especially especially nice if whoever did that would fess up and explain how they pulled it off." - Nobody?

"Finding classified documents … kind of cute" BWAHAHAHA

Also - Tankfuckers.
…is there context? I'm split as to whether I want context, or if this is one of those things that's scarier in your imagination

I definitely want to see more GAW/GOI interactions aside from just the Foundation and AWCY! I definitely want to see more of greenmanwalking, even just mentions. This was a fantastic article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really not sure what you're saying is wrong, here. I used my normal vocabulary when writing this article, and DolphinSlugchugger used its normal vocabulary. I'm having difficulty finding which words you thought were inappropriately large or esoteric; certainly, not every word used is an every day conversation word, but the goal of the article was not to discuss things that are entirely subjects of every day conversation.

Similarly, I'm not sure how to take the criticism that you have no clue what is happening if by your own admission it 'seems like a great idea' and 'keeps its ideas together, and makes sense' 'from what [you] read'. How much of this piece did you read? I can't write an article that makes sense before you read it; that is beyond my capabilities. If the article was too long for you to sit down and read all the way through (fully understandable; it is prodigious), that is why the article is in the form of several sections of written words that remain stable and can be read at different times, with periods of other types of activity between (any kind of activity you want).

If the article was too challenging to follow because of the vocabulary used or too long for it to be desirable to read all of it, I would love to know at which points you encountered those issues so I could look to sections to prune or lines to rephrase, but that doesn't seem to be the actual complaint being made, so, frankly, I'm just confused?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck this is fucking fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no clue at all what is happening in this SCP.

I mean, from what I read, it keeps its idea together, and makes sense, but still.

An SCP that just uses big words because the author wanted to use big words is not a great SCP at all.

However, from what I put together this seems like a great article and idea either way, and the format screw is nice as well.

Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article. It thematically makes sense all the way through, it never has a drop off in the writing, and it is constructed in a way that forces you to keep going (even though I stopped at the GOC portion because I was getting sleepy). The greenmanwalking files were the highlight for me: greenmanwalking became a very real character and grew with what we knew about Mr. Destiny. Everything after that point only strengthens the article.

+1 for Destiny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I will remember that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The farther I got into reading this, the more sucked in I got. What the whole article represents is beautiful, and you all did a wonderful job. Another excellent writing that shows what GAW is truly about. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should note that the tagging here is wrong, as this is an SCP and not a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I realize I've forgotten to comment on this? I love this piece from the depth of my soul. I love what Mr. Destiny represents. I love the sense of History he fucking exudes. It's like if a fucking Endless from the Sandman got to join GAW. This is just beautiful. I love this dog man. And I love the 2420 easter egg I bullied you into including.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: bones uses it/its pronouns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who checks new posts daily, I want to say - I really identify with Bones. I am glad that he was involved with this. I enjoyed reading this. Given things I've learned over the last forty eight hours I sincerely appreciate this.

Thank you. Please forgive me for not being more eloquent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i'm a lesbian but i am Really glad he's hot

Are you, perchance, a furry?

Anyways, I second all of this. Stellar work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay so as a casual fan who only catches up every few months… this is just incredible? It's such a labour of love and so much effort and impeccably put together everything. this is going to sty in my heart for a very long time

It's long enough that i'll just say some things which i really loved:
- the formatting. It's a really creative way to show this, i absolutely love the contrast between the formal prose style and the footnotes. The Help section has the real feel of an online group coming into some big sort of project and realising, we can do this, we're organizing this. Gamers Against Weed are
- the way you use the formatting for narrative progression? Like. the scp archive is clearly intended to make you think "this is a dangerous, infovorous keter"… i guess what i mean is you do the great literary foreshadowing! it's a masterfully crafted work!
- shout out to bones for the beautifully academic tone mixed with usernames like polaricecraps. it's SO funny that an alien satellite just does most of the admin here
- hey has lyris ever had involvement with GAW? is she still out there just drawing calliope? I hope she's happy
- again there's a real feeling of. GAW themselves coming into their own, their destiny? they're more than a bunch of friends dicking around on discord now.
- mr destiny himself is a great character. i'm a lesbian but i am Really glad he's hot
- dahlia being an integral part of this, maybe the most of a founding member, despite and because she's non magic, and because she connected with him on a personal level? This feels like a story about the importance of kindness and care and that's important to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoy this, though I will admit I'm particularly attached from reading it so much to reference in my other works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was a pleasure to have helped. :D
You both have done an excellent job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in author posts i believe the primary purpose is giving thanks. so, thank you to:

  • scarhaver, for having me as a coauthor, allowing me to work on such a wonderful article, and partaking with me lores both kept and forgotten.
  • decibelles, kinchtheknifeblade, monkeysky, et. al. from a discord server
  • you, for reading it.

i believe, however, author posts also lack a purpose of giving apologies. so, sorry to:

  • scarhaver, for all the elements removed. i was loathe to strike even a dot or tittle from the text, and there was much which was joyous to read which was sacrificed for pacing's sake.
  • silas greenmanwalking (fictional character) for dunking on you. i dont know if or when but i probably did. apologies.

thank you.
